topic: Warp Yarn Selection in Forming Fabrics
content: High tenacity and high shrinkage polyester monofilaments are commonly used as warp yarns in forming fabrics to improve fabric modulus, seam strength and dimensional stability during machine running.

topic: Soft Warp Usage on SSB Paper Side
content: Softer polyester warp yarns are used on the paper side of SSB fabrics to improve sheet-side smoothness and paper formation quality.
Polyamide weft yarns are commonly used in forming fabrics because of their superior abrasion resistance, which helps improve running life and wear performance.

topic: Machine Side High Shrinkage Weft in 1.5 Layer Fabrics
content: High shrinkage polyester and polyamide wefts are used on the machine side of 1.5-layer forming fabrics to improve edge curl control and structural stability.

topic: Filler Weft Function in 2.5 Layer Fabrics
content: The filler weft used in 2.5-layer forming fabrics improves fiber support and retention by increasing the support structure on the paper side surface.

topic: Binder Weft Function in SSB Fabrics
content: Binder wefts in SSB fabrics help lock the paper side and machine side layers together and reduce the risk of layer separation or delamination during machine operation.

topic: Meaning of Mesh in Forming Fabrics
content: Mesh describes the number of machine direction and cross machine direction yarns within a defined unit area of forming fabric and is an important parameter for fabric structure characterization.
Yarn diameter directly influences drainage rate, wear volume and surface characteristics of forming fabrics.

topic: Importance of Top Surface Open Area
content: Top surface open area is a better indicator of drainage potential in multilayer forming fabrics compared to CFM because it reflects actual drainage openings on the paper side surface.

topic: Limitations of CFM in Multilayer Fabrics
content: CFM is useful for evaluating single-layer fabrics but becomes less reliable for multilayer fabrics because center and bottom layers strongly influence air permeability values without directly representing drainage behavior.
Fiber Support Index evaluates the support characteristics of forming fabrics and helps compare formation support performance between single-layer, double-layer and triple-layer constructions.
G-weave forming fabrics provide more uniform drainage hole distribution, resulting in improved drainage uniformity and reduced twill marking compared to some E-weave and H-weave structures.
Triple-layer forming fabrics provide highly uniform paper-side support with consistent hole size distribution, improving sheet formation and reducing wire marking.

topic: Importance of Stacking in Forming Fabrics
content: Proper stacking alignment in multilayer forming fabrics improves drainage uniformity, paper-side smoothness and sheet support performance.

topic: Effect of Heat Setting on Stacking
content: Excessive or uneven heat setting can distort vertical yarn alignment and damage stacking quality by causing yarn migration between layers.
